articles.title: Scientists say this type of olive oil could boost brain power
articles.excerpt: Extra virgin olive oil might help protect your brain by working through your gut. A two-year study found that people who consumed it had better cognitive performance and more diverse gut bacteria than those using refined olive oil. Research

articles.title: The surprising reason you’re so productive one day and not the next

articles.title: Doing this throughout life may cut Alzheimer’s risk by 38%
articles.excerpt: A lifetime of mental stimulation—like reading, writing, and learning new skills—may help protect the brain as we age. People with the highest levels of cognitive enrichment had a much lower risk of Alzheimer’s and experienced symptoms years

articles.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don’t decline faster, study finds
articles.excerpt: Loneliness may quietly affect how well older adults remember things—but it might not be speeding up mental decline after all. A large European study tracking over 10,000 people for seven years found that those who felt lonelier started off

articles.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
articles.excerpt: Losing your sense of smell might signal Alzheimer’s far earlier than expected. Scientists found that immune cells in the brain actively destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. This damage begin

articles.title: Scientists say we’ve been treating Alzheimer’s all wrong
articles.excerpt: Alzheimer’s isn’t just one problem—it’s a tangled mix of biology, aging, and overall health. That’s why drugs targeting a single factor have fallen short, even as new treatments show modest benefits. Scientists are now pushing toward multi-

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut trigger behind ALS and dementia
articles.excerpt: A new study reveals that gut bacteria may play a key role in triggering ALS and frontotemporal dementia. Harmful sugars produced by these microbes can spark immune responses that damage the brain. This breakthrough explains why some genetic

articles.title: Cellular Drawstrings Seal the Developing Brain
articles.excerpt: Researchers identified a "purse-string" mechanism powered by actin and molecular motors that physically pulls the neural tube closed during early development.

articles.title: SSRIs May Trigger Tinnitus
articles.excerpt: A new study identifies a specific serotonin circuit that triggers the auditory system, explaining why SSRIs can worsen tinnitus and offering a new target for treatment.

articles.title: Napping Patterns Predict Mortality Risk
articles.excerpt: A 19-year study finds that frequent, long, and morning naps in older adults are associated with higher mortality, acting as a warning sign for underlying disease.

articles.title: Multiple Pregnancies May Shield the Female Brain From Stroke
articles.excerpt: A new study reveals that women with three or more live births have a reduced risk of stroke and covert vascular brain damage.

articles.title: Why the Brain Can’t Forget Earth’s Gravity in Space
articles.excerpt: A 20-year study reveals that astronauts' brains retain a "memory" of Earth's gravity, causing them to over-grip objects in space to prevent drops that aren't possible.

articles.title: Exercise and Ibuprofen May Shield Against Chemo Brain
articles.excerpt: A Phase II trial finds that walking and resistance exercise, along with low-dose ibuprofen, can protect cancer patients from "chemo brain" during treatment.

articles.title: The Molecular Code Behind Gut-Brain Communication
articles.excerpt: Neuroscientists have identified the specific bacterial sugars that trigger neurons in the gut to communicate with the brain, providing a mechanical explanation for the gut-brain axis.

articles.title: Gut Bacteria May Predict Parkinson’s Before Symptoms Appear
articles.excerpt: A major study identifies 176 gut microbe species that signal Parkinson’s risk years before symptoms, suggesting that gut health is a key early warning system for the disease.

articles.title: When Apologizing to Customers Hurts More Than It Helps
